segments.s634-g41: به + noun here marks the destination ('to/into Iran'); ایران is the place. بری is irregular in the provided sense list and does not match a normal verbal form there; in this line it functions as part of the verbal clause meaning roughly 'bring' or 'let be carried' (poetic/elliptical usage), so its precise lexical sense is not in the given options. باژ هندوستان is a noun phrase 'the baazh (cry/sound) of Hindustan' with the possessed ('هندوستان') following the possessee ('باژ'), a common poetic ordering.
segments.s634-g42: همه (quantifier) precedes the noun مرز 'frontiers/borders'. باشند is the 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive/optative form of 'to be' here used as a wish/command ('may they be' / 'let them be'). همداستان is the predicate adjective 'of one mind/like‑minded' and follows the copular verb, giving the sense 'may all the frontiers be of one accord.'
segments.s634-g43: همان is a demonstrative/determiner 'that/the same' before هدیه 'gift/tribute'. هند follows هدیه as the genitive possessor: 'the gift of India'. با is the preposition 'with', introducing باژ as an accompanying item; نیز 'also' emphasizes that this item is included among the gifts.
segments.s634-g44: Poetic listing introduced by repeated ز 'from/of' before each material: ز عود 'of oud (agarwood)', و 'and', ز عنبر 'of amber(gris)', ز هرگونه چیز 'of every kind of thing'. The repeated ز is a stylistic/poetic marker for a list of offerings; هرگونه modifies چیز 'any kind of thing'.
segments.s634-g45: بدو is an enclitic prepositional phrase 'to him' (indirect object) placed before the verb. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (the sense list given does not include the simple 'said' sense, so it is not matched there). بهرام is the subject following the verb (V–S order is common in heroic poetry). کای پادشا is a vocative 'O king' (کای is the old vocative particle, پادشا 'sovereign/king') addressing the addressee.
segments.s634-g46: بهند here functions adverbially/locatively ('in Hind/India' or 'to Hind' depending on context) and is followed by اندرون 'within/inside' to stress location. شاه و فرمانروا are predicate nouns 'kings and sovereigns' — the copula/verb is either the earlier گفت (introducing reported speech) or implied; word order places the location expression before the predicate nouns in this poetic line.
segments.s634-g47: به فرمان is an instrumental/agent phrase 'by the command (of)'. دارنده یزدان پاک is a genitive chain 'the holder/possessor of (the) pure Yezdān' — i.e., 'the pure Lord/God'; دارنده 'bearer/holder' governs the following genitive nouns, and پاک 'pure' is a qualifying adjective after یزدان.
segments.s634-g48: پی here means 'in pursuit of/after' (marking the object of the action or intent). اژدها را is the definite/accusative object marked by را. ببرم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form of بردن used here with future/import intention 'I will take/carry (away)'. ز خاک is the source phrase 'from the earth/ground'. The whole clause expresses the speaker's resolve: 'I will take the dragon from the ground.'
segments.s634-g17: فرستمش is the 1st‑person present/future form of فرستادن with the enclitic -ش (= 'him/it'), literally 'I send him' but here used as 'I will send him'. فردا is the temporal adverb 'tomorrow'. بر is a preposition meaning 'to/toward' (here 'to/against' the adversary) governing اژدها 'the dragon'. Word order is Verb+object+time+prepositional phrase in classical style.
segments.s634-g18: کزو is a relative/compound pronoun meaning 'from which / from whom', referring back to the dragon. بی‌گمانی is an adverb 'undoubtedly / without a doubt' modifying the verb. نیابد is the 3rd‑person present/subjunctive form of یابیدن with the negative prefix ن (here 'will not find / does not obtain'), and رها is a noun 'release / escape' functioning as the direct object of نیابد.
segments.s634-g19: نباشم is the 1st‑person negative form of بودن ('be / remain') meaning 'I shall not be' (classical Persian uses the present to state future/result). نکوهیده is a past participle used adjectivally 'blamed / censured'. کار اوی = 'his deed' (کار = deed/affair; اوی = his/her, a poetic possessive). The phrase as a whole says 'I will not be censured for his deed.'
segments.s634-g20: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when / as'. با + اژدها literally 'with the dragon' describes the opponent (i.e. 'in combat with the dragon'). خود is emphatic 'himself'. شود is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of شدن 'become'. جنگجوی is a predicate noun 'a warrior / fighter'. The clause means 'when he himself becomes a warrior against the dragon.'
segments.s634-g21: بگفت is a past tense narrative verb 'he said'. این = 'this' (the preceding words). و = 'and'. بهرام را (را marks the definite direct object) پیش خواند is a compound meaning 'called/summoned Bahram before him / forward' (پیش here 'into presence / forward', خواند used as 'summoned'). The sequence is 'he said this and summoned Bahram forward.'
segments.s634-g22: بسی is an intensive/quantifying word 'many / much'. داستان = 'tales / stories'. دلیران is the plural noun 'the brave ones / heroes'. براند here is the verb concluding the clause; in context it means 'he related / recounted' (a poetic use). Word order places the quantifier before the noun phrase and the verb at the end as is typical in classical Persian.
segments.s634-g23: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him, گفت = he said). یزدان پاک‌آفرین is a theophoric phrase 'God, the Pure‑Creator' used as the (implied) subject of the optative clause that follows — i.e. an invocation/blessing: 'May God, the Pure‑Creator ...'. Grammatically this places the divine name before the clause that prays for the addressee.
segments.s634-g24: ترا (here written as ت + را in the word list) is the 2nd‑person object 'you' (ت = 2nd‑person suffix, را = object marker). ایدر = 'here'. آورد is the past tense 'brought' (3rd‑person), with یزدان پاک‌آفرین as its implied subject: 'May God bring you here'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and ایران‌زمین = 'the land/realm of Iran'. The clause reads 'May God bring you here from the land of Iran.'
segments.s634-g33: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before its noun phrase (ز فرمانِ تو = 'from your command'). نگذرم is the first-person singular present/subjunctive with negative prefix ن- meaning 'I do not/will not leave/pass'; یک زمان is an adverbial phrase 'a single moment' placed after the verb for emphasis. The whole segment states the main negative commitment that the following clause limits.
segments.s634-g34: مگر introduces an exception ('unless'). The clause that follows has a poetically inverted order: predicate (بد) + copula (بود) + subject (گردش آسمان) = 'bad was the turning of the sky' — here expressing a hypothetical/unfavourable cosmic condition that would be the only exception to the prior refusal. بود is the past/copular form used in this counterfactual/hypothetical sense.
segments.s634-g35: بدو is the prepositional+dative phrase 'to him/to it' placed before the verb (بدو گفت = 'said to him'). گفت is the past verb 'said' (poetic past used narratively). که introduces the content clause. چندین is a quantifier 'several/many' and بلاست (poetically contracted from 'بلا است') asserts the existence of calamities — together: 'that there are many calamities.'
segments.s634-g36: بدین is a prepositional demonstrative ('to/in this') introducing the locative phrase; بومِ ما در is the prepositional phrase 'in this land of ours' (note the poetic order: demonstrative + bōm + ma + preposition در). یکی اژدهاست is another poetic inversion where یکی + noun + است (fused as اژدهاست) states 'there is one dragon' — in classical Persian the copula often attaches to the noun.
segments.s634-g37: به خشکی و دریا is an adverbial phrase 'by/over land and sea' (به used here as a circumstantial marker). همی is an older/perfective-continuous particle indicating habitual or ongoing action, and بگذرد is the third-person singular present/subjunctive/indicative 'passes/shall pass'. The line means 'it traverses both land and sea.'
segments.s634-g38: نهنگ is the subject 'the whale' (sea-monster). دم here means a brief moment/beat and stands in a genitive/possessive relation to آهنگ ('the moment/beat of the tune'); را marks آهنگ as the definite object, and بشمرد is the third-person singular present/subjunctive/indicative 'counts/ reckons'. Word order is subject + temporal/genitive phrase + object marker + verb: 'the whale counts the tune's breaths/moments.'
segments.s634-g39: توانی is second-person singular present 'you can' (ability/possibility). مگر here functions as a modal particle introducing a tentative/rhetorical question ('perhaps/can it be that...'). چاره‌ای is an indefinite object 'a remedy/means', and ساختن is the infinitive 'to make/devise' — together: 'can you perhaps devise a solution?'
segments.s634-g40: ازو is the ablative/agent pronoun 'from him/of him' (referring back to the foe). کشور هند is the noun phrase 'the realm/country of India' functioning as the object, and پرداختن is an infinitive verb that here means to effect/take/withdraw (an action toward that object) — the infinitive closes the purpose/complement begun by ساختن, so the whole segment reads 'to (bring about) taking the country of India from him.'
segments.s634-g9: مرا پشت بودی‌گر ایدر بدی is a compact conditional clause. مرا (here written in two pieces) indicates the person who would possess the backing ('for me' / 'I would have'); پشت is used metaphorically as 'support, backing, protector'; بودی‌گر is a fused conditional/optative form meaning 'if there had been' or 'had it been'; ایدر is the adverb 'here'; بدی is a noun meaning 'harm' or 'ill' and is the outcome implied by the rest. Word order places the possessor (مرا) before the possessed support (پشت) and closes the clause with the conditional particle بودی‌گر + location.
segments.s634-g10: This clause states the action that would follow the hypothetical backup. به قنوج marks direction 'toward Kannauj'; بر + کشوری means 'against / upon a country/realm' (بر governing an attack or action onto a realm); سر here is used metaphorically as 'chief/ruler' or 'head' of that realm; بدی again names the harm or mischief done. The phrase therefore describes raising oneself toward Kannauj and inflicting harm upon the realm's head.
segments.s634-g11: گر is the conditional 'if'; از نزد ما literally 'from our nearness' / 'from our presence' (از + نزد); سوی ایران شود places motion/direction 'toward Iran' with شود as the verbal copula or inchoative 'become/go' marking the event. The whole segment is a protasis: 'If he goes away from us toward Iran...'.
segments.s634-g12: ز here is the poetic preposition 'by/from' introducing the agent; بهرام is the agent's name; قنوج ویران شود is a passive-style clause 'Kannauj becomes ruined' with ویران 'ruined' and شود again marking the event. Together it says 'Kannauj will be ruined by Bahram.'
segments.s634-g13: چو here functions as a conditional 'if/when'; کهتر 'the lesser' (an inferior) is the subject; چنین 'thus/so' qualifies how the lesser is; باشد is the subjunctive/copula 'be' used to state the condition; و links to مهتر اوی where مهتر is 'the superior/chief' and اوی is the enclitic pronoun 'his/he' (possession/identification). So this segment means 'If the lesser is thus and his superior...'.
segments.s634-g14: نماند is the negative/resultative verb 'will not remain / there will not be left'; برین بوم ما = 'on this our land' (بر = on/upon, ین = the demonstrative element forming 'this' with بر); رنگ و بوی 'color and scent' are figurative for signs of prosperity or life. The clause states the consequence: 'there will be no color or scent left on this land of ours.'
segments.s634-g15: همه شب 'all night'; همی is an adverbial particle marking continuous or habitual action in older/poetic Persian ('kept on'); کار او 'his affair' (کار = business/plan, او = his); ساختم is the first-person past 'I made/did/arranged'. Together: 'All night I was occupied with his business.'
segments.s634-g16: یکی چاره دیگر is 'one other remedy/plan' (یکی = one/a certain, چاره = remedy/solution, دیگر = other/another); انداختم is the first-person past 'I threw/put' used idiomatically 'I devised/applied' (to 'cast' a plan). The line states 'I devised another plan.'
segments.s634-g49: ندانم is the negative present (ن + دانم) meaning "I do not know." که introduces a subordinate clause. The clause has subject او followed by the object/particle را (here marking topicalization/definiteness) and the noun نشیمن (dwelling/seat); کجاست is the interrogative adverb کجا plus the copula ست (= is), so word order is subject + را + predicate ("where is his dwelling").
segments.s634-g50: بباید is an impersonal/necessitative form ("it is necessary" / "one must"). نمودن is the infinitive "to show" used here as the main action; the indirect object is به من (to me), and راه راست is the direct object "the right/straight way." The literal order is "it must be shown to me the right way."
segments.s634-g51: فرستاد is the past simple "sent." شنگل is the sender's name (proper noun). یکی here is the indefinite article-like usage "a certain / a" and راه‌جوی is the noun "path-seeker / traveler," so the phrase means "(he) sent a certain path-seeker."
segments.s634-g52: که introduces a relative/purpose clause modifying راه‌جوی: نماید is a classical-present/subjunctive-like form from نمودن (here "to show/observe/find") used to express the action the seeker should perform. آن اژدها را is the definite object "that dragon" (را marks the object). بدوی is an archaic/poetic word placed after the verb as an epithet or adverbial modifier; its precise nuance is literary and obscure here but it colors the seeker or action poetically.
segments.s634-g53: همی is a frequentative/continuous particle (here "still/straight on"); رفت is the past "went/rode." با introduces accompaniment: نامور qualifies the following numeral phrase (famed), and سی سوار means "thirty riders" (adjective/epithet + noun). The whole segment = "he rode with thirty renowned horsemen."
segments.s634-g54: از marks origin or belonging ("from/among"); ایران here names the group (the Iranian forces/people). سواران is the plural "horsemen/riders." خنجرگزار is a compound epithet "dagger-bearing" (literally "those who carry daggers") modifying the riders. Word order is a genitive-like phrase giving their origin/identity: "dagger-bearing riders of Iran."
segments.s634-g55: همی (continuous particle) + تاخت (past "galloped/rushed"). تا introduces the limit "until," followed by پیش دریا ("in front of/before the sea") and رسید (past "arrived/reached"). The clause means "he rushed until he reached the sea."
segments.s634-g56: به تاریکی is an adverbial phrase here meaning "in/by the darkness" (به functioning adverbially). آن اژدها را is the definite object "that dragon" (را marks the object), and بدید is the past "saw." Word order is adverbial phrase + object + verb: "in the darkness he saw that dragon."
segments.s634-g73: تن اژدها is a genitive construction ('the body of the dragon') with no ezāfe—typical in older/poetic Persian. گشت is the past of گشت 'became/turned', so the clause means 'the dragon's body became ...'. زان is the archaic preposition 'from that', and تیر سست is 'a weak/feeble arrow' (noun + adjective), so the full segment reads 'the dragon's body became (weak) from that feeble arrow.'
segments.s634-g74: همی is an adverbial particle denoting continuous or ongoing action ('kept / was still'), here used with the past verb بشست to mean 'kept washing'. خاک را uses the object marker را to mark 'the earth' as the object. خون زهرش is a genitive/compound ('the blood of its poison' = 'its poisonous blood'), and بشست is the simple past 'washed' (3rd person). The clause therefore says the poisonous blood continued to wash the earth.
segments.s634-g75: یکی here is an indefinite 'someone / a certain one' performing the action. تیغ زهرآبگون is a noun followed by a compound adjective 'poison-green' (zahr-ābgun) describing the sword. برکشید is the past tense 'drew up / raised' (here: drew a weapon), so the segment means 'someone drew a poison-green sword.'
segments.s634-g76: به تندی is an adverbial phrase 'with swiftness / swiftly' (به + تندی 'haste'). دل اژدها is a genitive 'the dragon's heart' serving as the object, and بردرید is the past transitive 'he tore apart / cut' (3rd person). Grammatically the verb picks up the subject implied in the previous clause ('someone'), so the clause reads 'swiftly he tore the dragon's heart.'
segments.s634-g77: به here introduces the instrument ('with'); تیغ و تبرزین lists the instruments 'sword and battle-axe' joined by و. بزد is the past 'he struck / smote', and گردنش is 'its neck' with the possessive enclitic ش attached to گردن. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect but the sense is 'he struck its neck with sword and axe.'
segments.s634-g78: به خاک اندر افگند is a light-verb construction: به خاک 'onto the ground', اندر 'in/into' intensifies the locative, and افگند is the past 'he threw/cast'. بیجان تنش is 'his lifeless body' (بیجان + تن + possessive ش). Together: 'he cast his lifeless body down onto the earth.'
segments.s634-g79: به گردون is a prepositional phrase 'to the sky/heavens'; سرش is 'his head' (سر + possessive ش). سوی means 'toward' and شنگل (an obscure name/place) is its goal. کشید is the past 'drew / pulled / turned' acting on سرش — idiomatically 'he lifted/turned his head toward the heavens / toward Shengel.' Note: the supplied senses for کشید did not include 'draw/turn' so its sense is noted separately below.
segments.s634-g80: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; شاه is the subject 'the king'; آن is the determiner 'that'; سر اژدها را is the object ('that dragon's head') marked by را; and بدید is the simple past 'saw'. The clause therefore reads 'when the king saw that dragon's head.'
segments.s634-g81: برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/appeared' placed before its complements (poetic inversion). ز + هندوستان is the source phrase 'from Hindustan'. آفرین here functions as a noun (a wondrous creation or product) derived from the root of 'آفریدن' rather than as an exclamation.
segments.s634-g82: ز دادار = 'from the Creator' names the ultimate source; بر is the preposition 'upon/on' governing بوم ('homeland, soil'), and ایران‌زمین is the compound noun 'land of Iran'. Word order is standard poetic: source then location.
segments.s634-g83: که introduces a relative/clause ('that/who'). زاید is the verbal root meaning 'is born / comes into being' used generically here ('should be born' or 'is born'). برآن is the fused preposition + demonstrative (بر + آن) meaning 'on that', governing خاک 'soil'. چونین precedes the noun to mean 'such', so چونین سوار = 'such a rider'.
segments.s634-g84: The second که continues the relative idea ('who'). با اژدها = 'with the dragon'; سازد is third-person singular present/optative of ساختن 'to make/do' used idiomatically with کارزار to mean 'to make/wage war'. او is the pronoun 'he' (subject) placed after the verb in poetic order, and کارزار is the object 'battle/war'.
segments.s634-g85: برین is a contracted poetic form (بر + این) meaning 'on this/that'; برز بالا is an emphatic nominal phrase 'the lofty/high height' (literary 'high back/crest'). و این شاخ و یال lists body-parts 'and these horns and mane' — note the adjective/order: demonstrative (این) + noun.
segments.s634-g86: نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive used impersonally here to mean 'there is not' or 'none exists'. جز از = 'except (from/of)'; شهریارش is 'his king' (شهریار with a possessive/attributive), and همال means 'bearers/porters' — so the clause says nothing comparable exists there except the king's bearers.
segments.s634-g57: بزرگان ایران is a noun phrase 'the great ones / elders of Iran' (possessive/genitive sense: big men belonging to Iran). خروشان is a predicative adjective meaning 'roaring/uproarious'; شدند is the past plural of شدن (here 'became' or 'were'), so the clause literally 'the great men of Iran became roaring' (i.e., they cried out).
segments.s634-g58: وزان is a fused conjunctional phrase 'و + زان' meaning 'and from that / and because of that' linking to the previous clause. اژدها is the subject 'the dragon'; نیز is a postpositive particle 'also / too'; جوشان is a predicative adjective 'boiling / seething / furious'; شدند again is past plural 'became'. Word order (conjunction — subject — particle — predicate) is normal for poetic Persian.
segments.s634-g59: به بهرام گفتند is 'they said to Bahram' (به marks the indirect object). کای is the vocative particle 'O' (used in epic address); شهریار is the addressee title 'king / sovereign'. The clause frames reported speech that follows.
segments.s634-g60: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'; این را is the direct object 'this' with the object marker را. چو means 'like / as'; آن کرگ پیشین is a simile 'that former کرگ' (کرگ here is a dialectal bird/creature image); مدار is a prohibitive/imperative form in this context meaning 'do not make / do not act' (poetic/archaic usage). The clause is an imperative admonition: 'You, do not make this like that former krag.'
segments.s634-g61: به ایرانیان گفت is 'said to the Iranians' (به marks the indirect object). بهرام گرد here is best read as part of the reported speech: بهرام is the vocative/name and گرد is an imperative/ordering form from گردیدن / گرداندن meaning 'turn / gather / go about' — i.e., 'Bahrām, gather (yourselves)' or 'Bahrām said, "Gather"' (word order reflects reported speech in epic style).
segments.s634-g62: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that / for'; این را is the direct object 'this' (را = object marker); به دادار 'to the Dādār' (دادار is an epithet for the Creator/God, with به marking the recipient); باید is the modal 'must / ought'; سپرد is the verb 'to entrust / hand over' used after باید to mean 'should be entrusted' (here سپرد is the lexical verb in past stem form but functions after باید to express necessity).
segments.s634-g63: مرا‌گر is a concessive/conditional phrase 'مرا گر' = 'if it were (left) to me' or 'if I may say so / if it were up to me'. زمانه here means 'the age / fortune / the world' (the state of times). بدین is the compound 'ب + دین' meaning 'to/for this' or 'in this way' and combines with اژدهاست ('is the dragon') — so the clause says roughly 'if the world, in this way, belongs to this dragon' (i.e., if fortune favors this dragon). The final -است is the copula attached to اژدها.
segments.s634-g64: به مردی is a dative/advantage phrase 'by any man / through a man' (به + indefinite مردی). فزونی نگیرد uses the negative present-subjunctive/optative نگیرد (from گرفتن) with فزونی 'increase/advantage' — 'will not gain/increase'. نه کاست is a parallel negative 'nor (experience) decrease' — کاست here means 'diminution / lessening' (poetic noun), so the line states a balanced negation: neither gains from a man nor is diminished.
segments.s634-g25: که here is a subordinating conjunction introducing a purpose/relative clause ('so that, in order that'). هندوستان را is the definite direct object (را marks the object) placed before the verb, as in Persian SOV order. بشویی is the present-subjunctive/optative second-person singular ('you may wash/cleanse'), and ز بد = 'from/of evil' is a short prepositional phrase giving what is removed.
segments.s634-g26: چنان ... کز introduces a result/standard clause: چنان = 'so/thus' and کز is the classical fused form (که + از) meaning 'that/from which' (introducing the source or standard). ره نامداران ('the way/course of the famed') is the standard phrase placed before the verb, and سزد is an impersonal/present-form verb 'it befits / it is proper'.
segments.s634-g27: یکی here means 'a certain' (indefinite) modifying کار. پیش است is a predicative phrase 'is before / lies ahead' (پیش as an adverbial/postposition). با درد و رنج is an accompanying/instrumental phrase 'with pain and hardship' that qualifies the task; است is the present copula 'is'.
segments.s634-g28: به marks temporal/location: به آغاز رنج = 'at the beginning (there is) suffering' and به فرجام گنج = 'and at the outcome (there is) treasure'. Classical Persian often omits the ezâfe linking the nouns, so آغاز رنج = 'the beginning of suffering'. The two prepositional phrases are coordinated by و.
segments.s634-g29: چو این کرده باشی is a temporal/conditional clause: چو = 'when', کرده باشی is a perfect-subjunctive/past-subjunctive construction ('you have done'). زمانی here = 'for a time / for a while' (temporal adverb). مپای is an archaic/poetic negative imperative meaning 'do not remain/linger' (م- prefix negates the verb).
segments.s634-g30: به خشنودی من = 'with my pleasure/approval' (به marking accompaniment or manner; خشنودی takes the possessor من, classical style often omits ezâfe). برو is the simple imperative 'go' (2nd person singular). باز جای = 'back to (your) place/post' with باز functioning as an adverb ('back/again') modifying جای 'place/post'.
segments.s634-g31: به شنگل = 'to Shangal' (به marks the indirect object). چنین پاسخ آورد is a fixed phrase 'thus gave answer / replied' where آورد is the past tense 3rd-person singular of آوردن. شاه at the end is the subject placed finally for emphasis (poetic inversion: object/verb before subject).
segments.s634-g32: ک is the relativizer/conjunction 'that' introducing the subordinate clause. از رای تو بگذرم is a clause with بگذرم first-person ('I pass/leave/ignore'); classical Persian often uses the present-subjunctive form here. نیست راه ('there is no way') is an inverted existential: literal 'is not (there) a way' = 'there is no way'. Together: 'that there is no way that I depart from your counsel.'
segments.s634-g65: کمان را uses را as the definite direct-object marker: 'the bow.' به زه کرد is a causative/performative past construction (کرد) with به + زه meaning 'to string (with the string)' — literally 'made (it) with the string.' The clause then shifts to the coordinated action: و بگزید تیر, where بگزید (past of گزیدن 'to choose/fit') precedes its object تیر (poetic inversion omitting را), so: 'and he chose/nocked an arrow.'
segments.s634-g66: که introduces a relative clause modifying تیر ('an arrow which...'). پیکانش combines پیکان 'arrow' with the possessive suffix -ش 'its/his.' را again marks the definite object of داده (here a perfect/past participial sense 'had given / had applied'). بد modifies زهر as a compound 'bad/evil poison.' و شیر coordinates another noun; شیر here is literal 'lion' used figuratively (see note) to form 'lion's venom' or 'lion-like virulence' — the whole: 'whose arrow he had given foul poison and (the venom/ferocity of) a lion.'
segments.s634-g67: بران is an adverb meaning 'thereupon/then' and opens the line. اژدها is the subject 'the dragon.' تیرباران is a compound noun 'a shower of arrows' used as the event that befalls the dragon, and گرفت (past of گرفتن) here means 'took/received' — i.e. 'the dragon received a shower of arrows' (poetic, terse word order).
segments.s634-g68: چپ و راست is an adverbial phrase 'to the left and right / on both sides.' The sequence جنگ سواران گرفت inverts normal order for poetic effect: جنگ ('the battle' or 'combat') is the acting noun and سواران ('the horsemen') its patients; گرفت is used in a transitive/affected sense 'seized' or 'took hold of' — 'battle seized the horsemen (on left and right).' Note the omission of را for the object (common in poetry).
segments.s634-g69: به پولاد پیکان is an instrumental phrase 'by/with the steel of the arrow' (به here = 'by/with'). دهانش is 'his mouth' with the enclitic -ش. بدوخت is the past of دوخت 'to sew,' used here figuratively/quite literally: 'the steel of the arrow sewed his mouth (shut).' Word order places the instrument phrase before the main clause for emphasis.
segments.s634-g70: همی is a frequent poetic adverb (here 'then/thereupon' or continuous narration). خار can be understood metaphorically as 'a thorn' = 'an affliction' and is the subject. زان = از + آن 'from that' (referring back to the poison just mentioned). زهر او 'his poison' (او = he) is the source, and برفروخت is a verb meaning 'kindled/ignited' (here 'flared up' or 'was inflamed') rather than the literal 'sold.' Thus: 'from that poison a thorn (affliction) was kindled in him.'
segments.s634-g71: دگر here means 'again/another.' چار چوبه is a compound noun ('four-beamed' or a four-part wooden frame/club) functioning as the object; in context it denotes a heavy wooden blow. بزد is the past tense 'he struck/hit.' بر سرش is the locative phrase 'upon his head' with the enclitic -ش 'his.' The object-before-verb order and enclitic possessive are typical poetic patterns.
segments.s634-g72: فرو ریخت is a compound verb (فرو + ریخت) meaning 'fell down / poured down.' با introduces an accompanying circumstance 'with' (here 'with poison' — i.e. the blood is poisoned), so با زهر خون = 'blood with poison' or 'poisonous blood.' از is the preposition 'from,' and برش here means 'from his side/wound' (section of his body). Altogether: 'poisoned blood poured down from his wound.'
segments.s634-g1: یکی before a noun is the indefinite 'a certain'. اژدها is the noun 'dragon'. بود is the past copula 'was' (third‑person). بر + [خشک و آب] is a prepositional phrase 'over/on land and water' with خشک 'dry/land' and آب 'water' joined by و 'and'.
segments.s634-g2: به دریا is a prepositional phrase 'to/against the sea' marking the target of harm. بدی is used as the noun 'harm/evil' (predicative after the copula in the previous clause). گاه is an adverb 'at times/sometimes' and the second בר + آفتاب 'on the sun' completes the alternating pair ('now to the sea, now to the sun').
segments.s634-g3: همی is an adverb signalling continuous or iterative action ('kept/was continually'). درکشیدی is the past tense of درکشیدن 'to pull/draw' in the (poetic) second‑person form 'you pulled/kept pulling'. به introduces an instrumental phrase here ('by/with'); دم 'breath' is the instrument and ژنده پیل 'ragged elephant' is the noun phrase modifying that instrument.
segments.s634-g4: وزو is the fused/archaic form 'از او' (literally 'from it') meaning 'from that' (here marked as nonstandard in the supplied sense list). خاستی is a past form meaning 'arose' (classical narrative uses forms with -ی); موج is the subject 'wave', and دریای نیل is the genitive 'of the Nile'. The clause thus states that from that action the Nile's wave arose.
segments.s634-g5: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (the supplied senses list gives nouns, so no exact match there). شنگل is the speaker's name. به یاران خویش is the prepositional phrase 'to his companions' where خویش is the possessive 'his/own' (written here split into خوی + ش).
segments.s634-g6: بدان functions as an imperative 'know/let it be known' (from دانستن) and is written here as a combination of a prefix-like ب and دان. تیزهش (تیزه + ـش) literally 'its point/edge' is used idiomatically here to mean 'this matter' or 'this thing' to be communicated. رازداران خویش 'your/my (his) confidants' names the people who are to be informed; the final ش is the possessive suffix. The construction is an imperative/elliptical instruction to make that matter known to the trusted men.
segments.s634-g7: که introduces a content clause 'that/for'. من is the subject 'I'. زین (classical زین/ازین) is an archaic prepositional phrase 'because of/by this' (the supplied senses list does not include this usage, so it's marked accordingly). فرستاده is 'messenger' and شیرمرد is a compound 'lion‑man' = 'brave man / hero' used as predicative after the clause.
segments.s634-g8: گهی … گهی is the alternating adverbial pair 'at times ... at times ...'. شادمانم is the adjective shadman 'joyful' with the first‑person suffix '‑am' ('I am joyful'). پر ز + noun is a common construction meaning 'full of' (پر 'full' + ز 'of'), so پر ز درد means 'full of pain/sorrow' (درد here is emotional suffering).

sentences.s634-s21: Bring to Iran the cry/sound of Hindustan; may all the borderlands be of one accord.
sentences.s634-s22: The same gift/tribute from Hind (India), with its baazh (cry/sound) as well, — of oud and of amber and of every kind of thing.
sentences.s634-s23: To him Bahram said, 'O king! — in Hind (India) there are inside kings and sovereigns.'
sentences.s634-s24: By the command of the pure Lord God, I will take the dragon from the earth.
sentences.s634-s9: I will send him tomorrow against the dragon, from which he will, without doubt, find no release.
sentences.s634-s10: I shall not be blamed for his deed when he himself becomes a fighter against the dragon.
sentences.s634-s11: He said this and summoned Bahram forward; he recounted many tales of the brave.
sentences.s634-s12: He said to him, 'May God the Pure‑Creator bring you here from the land of Iran.'
sentences.s634-s17: I will not depart from your command for even a single moment, unless the turning of the heavens were ill.
sentences.s634-s18: Shangal said to him that there are many calamities; in this land of ours there is one dragon.
sentences.s634-s19: It passes by land and sea; the whale counts the moments of the rhythm.
sentences.s634-s20: Can you, perhaps, devise any means to take the realm of India from him?
sentences.s634-s5: If I had a backing here, you would raise yourself toward Kannauj and bring harm upon the realm's ruler.
sentences.s634-s6: If he leaves our presence and goes toward Iran, Kannauj will be laid waste by Bahram.
sentences.s634-s7: If the lesser is thus and his superior is not there, our land will have no color or scent left.
sentences.s634-s8: All night long I contrived his affairs; I devised another plan.
sentences.s634-s25: I do not know where his dwelling is; the right way must be shown to me.
sentences.s634-s26: Shangal sent a path-seeker to seek out that dragon.
sentences.s634-s27: He rode with thirty renowned horsemen — dagger-bearing riders of Iran.
sentences.s634-s28: He dashed on until he reached the sea; in the darkness he saw that dragon.
sentences.s634-s37: The dragon's body grew weak from that feeble arrow; its poisonous blood kept washing the earth.
sentences.s634-s38: Someone drew a poison-green sword; with swiftness he tore the dragon's heart.
sentences.s634-s39: He struck its neck with sword and battle-axe and cast its lifeless body down upon the earth.
sentences.s634-s40: He lifted his head to the heavens and turned it toward Shengel; when the king saw that dragon's head...
sentences.s634-s41: From Hindustan there arose a marvel — a creation from the Creator — upon the soil of Iran.
sentences.s634-s42: That such a rider should be born on that soil — one who would wage war with the dragon.
sentences.s634-s43: On that lofty height and on these horns and mane there is none except the bearers of his king.
sentences.s634-s29: The great men of Iran shouted in uproar, and from that the dragon too grew seething.
sentences.s634-s30: They said to Bahram, 'O king, do not make this like that former krag.'
sentences.s634-s31: Bahram said to the Iranians, 'Gather (or turn), for this ought to be entrusted to the Creator.'
sentences.s634-s32: If, for me, the age belongs to this dragon, it will neither gain by any man nor suffer diminution.
sentences.s634-s13: that you cleanse India of evil, in such a way that it befits the course of the renowned.
sentences.s634-s14: A certain task lies ahead with pain and suffering: hardship at the outset and treasure at the end.
sentences.s634-s15: When you have done this, do not linger a while; with my approval go back to your post.
sentences.s634-s16: To Shangal the king thus replied: that there is no way for me to depart from your counsel.
sentences.s634-s33: He strung the bow with its string and fitted an arrow — an arrow whose point he had anointed with foul poison and lion's venom.
sentences.s634-s34: Thereupon the dragon was showered with arrows; left and right the battle seized the horsemen.
sentences.s634-s35: The arrow's steel point sewed his mouth shut; from that poison a thorn (i.e. an affliction) flared up in him.
sentences.s634-s36: Again he struck the four-beamed staff upon his head; with poison, blood poured from his wound.
sentences.s634-s1: There was a certain dragon over land and water; it did harm, now to the sea, now to the sun.
sentences.s634-s2: You kept pulling (it) with the breath of a ragged elephant, and from that the wave of the Nile rose.
sentences.s634-s3: Thus Shengel said to his companions: tell this to your (or my) confidants.
sentences.s634-s4: For I, because of this messenger, am a man of lion‑like valor: sometimes I am joyful, sometimes full of pain.
